Page 5 of 5

📚 Placeholder for Pippi Longstocking - Chapter 8, Part 5

This page will contain approximately 500 words from the chapter when book text is integrated. The formatting is ready: 11px font, line-height 1.2, ~5 pages per chapter.

Page 9

Reading Comprehension

Answer these questions about today's chapter:

Question 1:

What did Doctor Dolittle do when he first learned that he could talk to animals?

0 / 20 words

Question 2:

Why do you think the animals decided to come and live with Doctor Dolittle?

0 / 20 words

Question 3:

Do you think it was a good idea for Doctor Dolittle to start talking to the animals? Why or why not?

0 / 20 words

Page 10

Journal Reflection

[Personal reflection prompt related to Chapter 8]

Page 11

Informational Text

The Amazing World of Animal Communication

Part 1 of 2

In the story of Dr. Dolittle, we learn about the amazing ability of animals to communicate with each other and with humans. Animals use a variety of methods to express their thoughts, feelings, and needs, from body language and vocalizations to even the ability to learn human speech. This incredible world of animal communication is something we can all learn more about.

Page 12

The Amazing World of Animal Communication

Part 2 of 2

By understanding how animals communicate, we can better appreciate the complexity of the natural world and the unique ways in which different species interact. From the intricate dances of bees to the complex social structures of elephants, the ways in which animals communicate are truly fascinating. As we read about Dr. Dolittle's adventures, we can explore this topic further and gain a deeper understanding of the animal kingdom.

Page 13

Question 1 of 3

1. What are some of the ways that animals communicate with each other?

0 / 15 words

Page 14

Question 2 of 3

2. How does the ability of animals to learn human speech help them in the story?

0 / 15 words

Page 15

Question 3 of 3

3. What are some examples of animal communication that you have observed in your own life?

0 / 15 words

Page 16

Grammar Workshop

Compound Sentences

What is it?
Proper nouns are the specific names of people, places, or things. In this story, we see many proper nouns used to describe the different animals and characters.

Example from today's reading:
"Some examples of proper nouns in the story include 'Dr. Dolittle', 'Jip the Dog', and 'Polynesia the Parrot'."

Your Turn:

Write three compound sentences about today's chapter using different coordinating conjunctions.

Page 17

Informative Writing

Prompt:

Describe what life was like for Dorothy in Kansas before the cyclone. Include at least 3 specific details.

Remember: Write a paragraph explaining something from the story. Include clear details and examples.

Checklist: Did you include a topic sentence? Supporting details? A conclusion?

Page 18

Language Skills

Root Words and Affixes

Learn:
Many words are built from roots (base words) with prefixes (before) and suffixes (after) added.

Activity:

Break down vocabulary words into their parts: prefix + root + suffix. Example: uncomfortable = un + comfort + able

Excellent Work!

You've completed all 11 pages of Lesson 128!

Page 19

Great Job!

You've completed Lesson 128! Come back tomorrow to continue reading The Railway Children.

Page 5 of 5

📚 Placeholder for Pippi Longstocking - Chapter 9, Part 5

This page will contain approximately 500 words from the chapter when book text is integrated. The formatting is ready: 11px font, line-height 1.2, ~5 pages per chapter.

Page 9

Reading Comprehension

Answer these questions about today's chapter:

Question 1:

What was the name of the parrot that Dr. Dolittle taught to speak?

0 / 20 words

Question 2:

Why did the parrot want to learn how to speak human language?

0 / 20 words

Question 3:

Do you think it was a good idea for Dr. Dolittle to teach the parrot how to speak? Why or why not?

0 / 20 words

Page 10

Journal Reflection

[Personal reflection prompt related to Chapter 9]

Page 11

Informational Text

The Wondrous World of Doctor Dolittle's Animal Friends

Part 1 of 2

In the whimsical tale of Doctor Dolittle, we discover a remarkable man who possesses the extraordinary ability to communicate with all creatures, from the smallest insects to the mightiest beasts. As the good doctor embarks on a grand adventure, he forms deep connections with his animal companions, learning about their unique perspectives and the challenges they face in the world.

Page 12

The Wondrous World of Doctor Dolittle's Animal Friends

Part 2 of 2

Through his interactions with these fascinating animals, Doctor Dolittle not only helps them but also gains a profound understanding of the natural world. His adventures showcase the importance of respecting and appreciating the diverse forms of life that share our planet, and the power of compassionate communication to bridge the divide between humans and the animal kingdom.

Page 13

Question 1 of 3

1. What special ability does Doctor Dolittle have, and how does it help him in his adventures?

0 / 15 words

Page 14

Question 2 of 3

2. How do the doctor's animal friends contribute to the story and help him on his journey?

0 / 15 words

Page 15

Question 3 of 3

3. What lessons about the natural world and the importance of communication can readers learn from Doctor Dolittle's experiences?

0 / 15 words

Page 16

Grammar Workshop

Action vs. Linking Verbs

What is it?
A compound sentence is made up of two independent clauses joined by a coordinating conjunction (such as 'and', 'but', or 'or'). This allows you to combine related ideas and create more complex and engaging sentences.

Example from today's reading:
"In the book, Doctor Dolittle could talk to animals, and he used this ability to help them in their time of need."

Your Turn:

Find five verbs in today's reading and label each as action or linking.

Page 17

Narrative Writing

Prompt:

Imagine you are Aunt Em in the cellar. Write about what you're thinking as the storm passes overhead.

Remember: Write a short story or scene with a clear beginning, middle, and end. Include descriptive details and dialogue if appropriate.

Checklist: Did you include a topic sentence? Supporting details? A conclusion?

Page 18

Language Skills

Idioms and Figurative Language

Learn:
Idioms are phrases that don't mean exactly what the words say (like 'raining cats and dogs').

Activity:

Identify any idioms or figurative language in today's chapter and explain what they really mean.

Excellent Work!

You've completed all 11 pages of Lesson 129!

Page 19

Great Job!

You've completed Lesson 129! Come back tomorrow to continue reading The Railway Children.

Day 130: Day 130 Assessment - 3rd Grade BedrockELA

Page 1

Lesson 130

Assessment Day

The Railway Children

Page 2

Assessment Instructions

Today is an assessment day! You'll be tested on:

  • Vocabulary: 10 words from Days 126-130
  • Reading Comprehension: Questions about the chapters
  • Grammar: Adjectives and Adjective Order
  • Writing: Opinion Writing

Time needed: About 45-60 minutes

Tip: Take your time and do your best work!

Page 3

Vocabulary Assessment

Write the definition for each word. Use complete sentences.

1. delightful

2. humble

3. radiant

4. miracle

5. terrific

6. solemn

7. earnest

8. whisper

9. struggle

10. comfort

Page 4

Reading Comprehension Test

Answer these questions about the chapters you read this week:

Question 1:

[Comprehension question about this week's chapters]

Question 2:

[Comprehension question about this week's chapters]

Question 3:

[Comprehension question about this week's chapters]

Question 4:

[Comprehension question about this week's chapters]

Question 5:

[Comprehension question about this week's chapters]

Page 5

Grammar Assessment

Adjectives and Adjective Order

Write three sentences with multiple adjectives describing characters or places from the chapter.

Page 6

Writing Assessment

Opinion Writing

Prompt:

Should Dorothy have tried harder to get into the cellar, or was it okay that she stopped to get Toto? Defend your opinion.

Requirements: Write a paragraph stating your opinion with at least 2-3 reasons supporting your view. Include a topic sentence and conclusion.

Page 7

Assessment Complete!

Great work on your assessment! You've completed 5 full days of The Railway Children.

See you on Day 131!

Page 9

Reading Comprehension

Answer these questions about today's chapter:

Question 1:

What did the animals say when they first met Dr. Dolittle?

0 / 20 words

Question 2:

Why did the animals decide to help Dr. Dolittle learn their languages?

0 / 20 words

Question 3:

Do you think it was a good idea for Dr. Dolittle to learn how to talk to animals? Why or why not?

0 / 20 words

Page 10

Journal Reflection

[Personal reflection prompt related to Chapter 11]

Page 11

Informational Text

The Wonders of the Animal Kingdom: Communication and Adventure in 'The Story of Dr. Dolittle'

Part 1 of 2

In 'The Story of Dr. Dolittle,' readers are introduced to a remarkable doctor who can communicate with animals. Through his adventures, we learn about the diverse ways animals communicate and the importance of understanding their language.

Page 12

The Wonders of the Animal Kingdom: Communication and Adventure in 'The Story of Dr. Dolittle'

Part 2 of 2

As Dr. Dolittle explores the animal kingdom, he discovers that each species has its own unique form of communication, from the intricate dance of the bees to the complex vocalizations of the monkeys. These discoveries not only broaden our understanding of the natural world but also inspire us to be more attentive to the needs and experiences of the creatures around us.

Page 13

Question 1 of 3

1. How does Dr. Dolittle's ability to communicate with animals help him in his adventures?

0 / 15 words

Page 14

Question 2 of 3

2. What are some of the different ways animals communicate with each other?

0 / 15 words

Page 15

Question 3 of 3

3. Why is it important for humans to understand and respect the communication methods of animals?

0 / 15 words

Page 16

Grammar Workshop

Past Progressive Tense

What is it?
A compound sentence is a sentence that contains two independent clauses joined by a coordinating conjunction (e.g., and, but, or, nor, for, so, yet).

Example from today's reading:
"Dr. Dolittle could talk to animals, and he used this ability to help them in his adventures."

Your Turn:

Write three sentences using the past progressive tense to describe things happening in today's chapter.

Page 17

Informative Writing

Prompt:

Describe what life was like for Dorothy in Kansas before the cyclone. Include at least 3 specific details.

Remember: Write a paragraph explaining something from the story. Include clear details and examples.

Checklist: Did you include a topic sentence? Supporting details? A conclusion?

Page 18

Language Skills

Similes and Metaphors

Learn:
A simile compares two things using 'like' or 'as'. A metaphor says one thing IS another thing.

Activity:

Find the simile in today's reading or create your own simile about the story.

Excellent Work!

Page 9

Reading Comprehension

Answer these questions about today's chapter:

Question 1:

What kind of animal did Polynesia the parrot teach Dr. Dolittle how to talk to?

0 / 20 words

Question 2:

Why do you think Polynesia the parrot was able to teach Dr. Dolittle to talk to animals?

0 / 20 words

Question 3:

Do you think it was a good idea for Dr. Dolittle to learn how to talk to animals? Why or why not?

0 / 20 words

Page 10

Journal Reflection

[Personal reflection prompt related to Chapter 12]

Page 11

Informational Text

Communicating with Animals

Part 1 of 2

Have you ever wished you could talk to animals? In the story of Dr. Dolittle, the main character has a special gift - he can understand and speak the languages of animals. This allows him to go on amazing adventures and learn all about the animal world. Communication is so important, not just for people, but for all living creatures.

Page 12

Communicating with Animals

Part 2 of 2

Animals use a variety of methods to communicate, like sounds, body language, and even scents. Listening and understanding their messages helps Dr. Dolittle connect with the animals and learn about their needs and experiences. Developing this skill of communication opens up a whole new world of understanding and friendship.

Page 13

Question 1 of 3

1. What are some ways that animals communicate?

0 / 15 words

Page 14

Question 2 of 3

2. How does Dr. Dolittle's ability to talk to animals help him on his adventures?

0 / 15 words

Page 15

Question 3 of 3

3. Why is communication important between people and animals?

0 / 15 words

Page 16

Grammar Workshop

Prepositional Phrases

What is it?
Adjectives are words that describe nouns. They help add more detail and information about the things we are talking about.

Example from today's reading:
"In the story, Dr. Dolittle has a kind and gentle way of speaking to the animals."

Your Turn:

Find three prepositional phrases in today's reading and circle the preposition in each one.

Page 17

Narrative Writing

Prompt:

Imagine you are Aunt Em in the cellar. Write about what you're thinking as the storm passes overhead.

Remember: Write a short story or scene with a clear beginning, middle, and end. Include descriptive details and dialogue if appropriate.

Checklist: Did you include a topic sentence? Supporting details? A conclusion?

Page 18

Language Skills

Context Clues

Learn:
Use surrounding words to figure out the meaning of an unfamiliar word.

Activity:

Find an unfamiliar word in the text and use context clues to determine its meaning before looking it up.

Excellent Work!

  • Read from A Little Princess
  • Learn 2 new vocabulary words
  • Read informational text: "Related Non-Fiction"
  • Practice grammar: Compound Sentences
  • Write: Opinion Writing
  • Explore: Root Words and Affixes

Time needed: About 45-60 minutes

Page 3

Vocabulary Words

speculation

lavish

Reading: Pippi Longstocking

Chapter 13

Page 8

Page 5 of 5

📚 Placeholder for Pippi Longstocking - Chapter 13, Part 5

This page will contain approximately 500 words from the chapter when book text is integrated. The formatting is ready: 11px font, line-height 1.2, ~5 pages per chapter.

Page 9

Reading Comprehension

Answer these questions about today's chapter:

Question 1:

What does Dr. Dolittle do when he discovers that the monkeys have stolen his medicine?

0 / 20 words

Question 2:

Why do you think the monkeys wanted to steal Dr. Dolittle's medicine?

0 / 20 words

Question 3:

How do you think Dr. Dolittle should have handled the situation with the monkeys differently?

0 / 20 words

Page 10

Journal Reflection

[Personal reflection prompt related to Chapter 13]

Page 11

Informational Text

The Language of Animals

Part 1 of 2

Have you ever wondered how animals communicate with each other? In the story of Dr. Dolittle, we learn that the doctor has a special ability to talk to animals and understand their language. This allows him to go on amazing adventures and help all kinds of creatures. Animals use a variety of sounds, body language, and even scents to communicate their needs, feelings, and messages to one another.

Page 12

The Language of Animals

Part 2 of 2

While we may not be able to speak 'animal' like Dr. Dolittle, we can still learn a lot about how animals communicate. By observing their behaviors and sounds, we can begin to understand what they are trying to say. This helps us appreciate the incredible diversity of life on our planet and the important role that animals play in our world.

Page 13

Question 1 of 3

1. What is the special ability that Dr. Dolittle has?

0 / 15 words

Page 14

Question 2 of 3

2. How do animals communicate with each other?

0 / 15 words

Page 15

Question 3 of 3

3. Why is it important to learn about how animals communicate?

0 / 15 words

Page 16

Grammar Workshop

Compound Sentences

What is it?
Proper nouns are the specific names of people, places, or things. They should always be capitalized, like 'Dr. Dolittle' and 'Hugh Lofting'.

Example from today's reading:
"In the story, we read about the famous animal doctor, Dr. Dolittle, and the author who created him, Hugh Lofting."

Your Turn:

Write three compound sentences about today's chapter using different coordinating conjunctions.

Page 17

Opinion Writing

Prompt:

Should Dorothy have tried harder to get into the cellar, or was it okay that she stopped to get Toto? Defend your opinion.

Remember: Write a paragraph stating your opinion with at least 2-3 reasons supporting your view. Include a topic sentence and conclusion.

Checklist: Did you include a topic sentence? Supporting details? A conclusion?

Page 18

Language Skills

Root Words and Affixes

Learn:
Many words are built from roots (base words) with prefixes (before) and suffixes (after) added.

Activity:

Break down vocabulary words into their parts: prefix + root + suffix. Example: uncomfortable = un + comfort + able

Excellent Work!

You've completed all 11 pages of Lesson 133!

Page 19

Great Job!

You've completed Lesson 133! Come back tomorrow to continue reading The Railway Children.

Reading: Pippi Longstocking

Page 9

Reading Comprehension

Answer these questions about today's chapter:

Question 1:

Why did the animals decide to go to Africa to find the Great Green Sea Snail?

0 / 20 words

Question 2:

How did the animals feel when they realized the Sea Snail was not in Africa?

0 / 20 words

Question 3:

Do you think the animals should have made the long journey to Africa? Why or why not?

0 / 20 words

Page 10

Journal Reflection

[Personal reflection prompt related to Chapter 14]

Page 11

Informational Text

The Amazing World of Animal Communication

Part 1 of 2

Have you ever wondered how animals talk to each other? In the story of Dr. Dolittle, we learn that the doctor can understand and speak the languages of all the animals. This is a special skill that allows him to communicate with his animal friends and go on exciting adventures with them. Animals use a variety of ways to communicate, like body language, sounds, and even scents.

Page 12

The Amazing World of Animal Communication

Part 2 of 2

For example, birds use their songs to mark their territory and attract mates. Dolphins click and whistle to find each other in the ocean. Ants leave chemical trails to show the way to food. All these forms of animal communication are fascinating to learn about. Imagine being able to talk to the animals like Dr. Dolittle can!

Page 13

Question 1 of 3

1. What are some ways that animals communicate with each other?

0 / 15 words

Page 14

Question 2 of 3

2. How does Dr. Dolittle's ability to speak animal languages help him go on adventures?

0 / 15 words

Page 15

Question 3 of 3

3. Why is it important for animals to be able to communicate?

0 / 15 words

Page 16

Grammar Workshop

Action vs. Linking Verbs

What is it?
Proper nouns are the names of specific people, places, or things. They should always be capitalized.

Example from today's reading:
"In the story, the main character's name is Dr. Dolittle, which is a proper noun."

Your Turn:

Find five verbs in today's reading and label each as action or linking.

Page 17

Informative Writing

Prompt:

Describe what life was like for Dorothy in Kansas before the cyclone. Include at least 3 specific details.

Remember: Write a paragraph explaining something from the story. Include clear details and examples.

Checklist: Did you include a topic sentence? Supporting details? A conclusion?

Page 18

Language Skills

Idioms and Figurative Language

Learn:
Idioms are phrases that don't mean exactly what the words say (like 'raining cats and dogs').

Activity:

Identify any idioms or figurative language in today's chapter and explain what they really mean.

Excellent Work!

You've completed all 11 pages of Lesson 134!

Page 19

Great Job!

You've completed Lesson 134! Come back tomorrow to continue reading The Railway Children.

Day 135: Day 135 Assessment - 3rd Grade BedrockELA

Page 1

Lesson 135

Assessment Day

The Railway Children

Page 2

Assessment Instructions

Today is an assessment day! You'll be tested on:

  • Vocabulary: 10 words from Days 131-135
  • Reading Comprehension: Questions about the chapters
  • Grammar: Adjectives and Adjective Order
  • Writing: Narrative Writing

Time needed: About 45-60 minutes

Tip: Take your time and do your best work!

Page 3

Vocabulary Assessment

Write the definition for each word. Use complete sentences.

1. delightful

2. humble

3. radiant

4. miracle

5. terrific

6. solemn

7. earnest

8. whisper

9. struggle

10. comfort

Page 4

Reading Comprehension Test

Answer these questions about the chapters you read this week:

Question 1:

[Comprehension question about this week's chapters]

Question 2:

[Comprehension question about this week's chapters]

Question 3:

[Comprehension question about this week's chapters]

Question 4:

[Comprehension question about this week's chapters]

Question 5:

[Comprehension question about this week's chapters]

Page 5

Grammar Assessment

Adjectives and Adjective Order

Write three sentences with multiple adjectives describing characters or places from the chapter.

Page 6

Writing Assessment

Narrative Writing

Prompt:

Imagine you are Aunt Em in the cellar. Write about what you're thinking as the storm passes overhead.

Requirements: Write a short story or scene with a clear beginning, middle, and end. Include descriptive details and dialogue if appropriate.

Page 7

Assessment Complete!

Great work on your assessment! You've completed 5 full days of The Railway Children.

See you on Day 136!

Reading Comprehension

Answer these questions about today's chapter:

Question 1:

What did the Doctor decide to do after he heard the pigeon's message?

0 / 20 words

Question 2:

Why do you think the Doctor wanted to travel to Africa to see the monkeys?

0 / 20 words

Question 3:

Do you think the Doctor made the right decision to go to Africa? Why or why not?

0 / 20 words

Page 10

Journal Reflection

[Personal reflection prompt related to Chapter 16]

Page 11

Informational Text

The Incredible World of Animal Communication

Part 1 of 2

Did you know that animals can communicate with each other in amazing ways? From the high-pitched calls of birds to the deep rumbles of elephants, animals have developed unique ways to share information, express their feelings, and even coordinate their actions. In the story of Dr. Dolittle, we see how the doctor is able to understand and speak the languages of the animals he encounters, opening up a whole new world of adventure and discovery.

Page 12

The Incredible World of Animal Communication

Part 2 of 2

Communication is essential for animals to survive and thrive in their environments. They use a variety of sounds, body language, and even scents to convey important messages. For example, wolves use howls to mark their territory and coordinate their pack, while bees perform intricate dances to share the location of food sources. Exploring the fascinating world of animal communication can help us better understand the incredible diversity of life on our planet.

Page 13

Question 1 of 3

1. What are some of the ways that animals communicate with each other?

0 / 15 words

Page 14

Question 2 of 3

2. How does the ability to understand animal languages help Dr. Dolittle in his adventures?

0 / 15 words

Page 15

Question 3 of 3

3. Why is communication important for animals in their daily lives?

0 / 15 words

Page 16

Grammar Workshop

Past Progressive Tense

What is it?
Compound sentences are made up of two or more independent clauses joined by a conjunction, such as 'and', 'but', or 'so'. This helps create more complex and interesting sentences.

Example from today's reading:
"In the story, Dr. Dolittle says, 'I can talk to the animals, and they can talk to me.'"

Your Turn:

Write three sentences using the past progressive tense to describe things happening in today's chapter.

Page 17

Opinion Writing

Prompt:

Should Dorothy have tried harder to get into the cellar, or was it okay that she stopped to get Toto? Defend your opinion.

Remember: Write a paragraph stating your opinion with at least 2-3 reasons supporting your view. Include a topic sentence and conclusion.

Checklist: Did you include a topic sentence? Supporting details? A conclusion?

Page 18

Language Skills

Similes and Metaphors

Learn:
A simile compares two things using 'like' or 'as'. A metaphor says one thing IS another thing.

Activity:

Find the simile in today's reading or create your own simile about the story.

Excellent Work!

You've completed all 11 pages of Lesson 136!

Page 19

Great Job!

You've completed Lesson 136! Come back tomorrow to continue reading The Railway Children.

Book: A Little Princess by Frances Hodgson Burnett

Page 2 of 5

Page 3 of 5

This page will contain approximately 500 words from the chapter when book text is integrated. The formatting is ready: 11px font, line-height 1.2, ~5 pages per chapter.

Page 4 of 5

📚 Placeholder for Pippi Longstocking - Chapter 17, Part 4

This page will contain approximately 500 words from the chapter when book text is integrated. The formatting is ready: 11px font, line-height 1.2, ~5 pages per chapter.

Page 8

Page 5 of 5

📚 Placeholder for Pippi Longstocking - Chapter 17, Part 5

This page will contain approximately 500 words from the chapter when book text is integrated. The formatting is ready: 11px font, line-height 1.2, ~5 pages per chapter.

Page 9

Reading Comprehension

Answer these questions about today's chapter:

Question 1:

What kind of animal does Dr. Dolittle teach to read and write?

0 / 20 words

Question 2:

Why does Dr. Dolittle decide to teach the animal to read and write?

0 / 20 words

Question 3:

Do you think teaching animals to read and write is a good idea? Why or why not?

0 / 20 words

Page 10

Journal Reflection

[Personal reflection prompt related to Chapter 17]

Page 11

Informational Text

Unique Animal Languages

Part 1 of 2

Did you know that animals have their own unique ways of communicating? From barks and growls to body language and scent, animals use all sorts of methods to talk to each other. In the story of Dr. Dolittle, the main character can understand and speak to animals, which allows him to go on amazing adventures. Let's explore how some animals communicate!

Page 12

Unique Animal Languages

Part 2 of 2

Many animals use sounds to communicate. Dogs bark to warn others of danger or to show excitement. Whales sing long, complex songs to attract mates and mark their territory. Even insects like crickets and cicadas make noises to find each other. Other animals rely on body language, like the way a cat arches its back when it's angry or a bird fans its feathers to attract a mate. Scents are also an important way that animals communicate, leaving behind smells to mark their territory or signal their mood.

Page 13

Question 1 of 3

1. What are some ways that animals communicate with each other?

0 / 15 words

Page 14

Question 2 of 3

2. How does the ability to talk to animals help Dr. Dolittle go on adventures?

0 / 15 words

Page 15

Question 3 of 3

3. Why is communication important for animals?

0 / 15 words

Page 16

Grammar Workshop

Prepositional Phrases

What is it?
Adjectives are words that describe nouns. They can tell us about the size, color, shape, or other qualities of a person, place, or thing.

Example from today's reading:
"In the story, Dr. Dolittle meets many interesting animals, like the clever parrot and the friendly dog."

Your Turn:

Find three prepositional phrases in today's reading and circle the preposition in each one.

Page 17

Informative Writing

Prompt:

Describe what life was like for Dorothy in Kansas before the cyclone. Include at least 3 specific details.

Remember: Write a paragraph explaining something from the story. Include clear details and examples.

Checklist: Did you include a topic sentence? Supporting details? A conclusion?

Page 18

Language Skills

Context Clues

Learn:
Use surrounding words to figure out the meaning of an unfamiliar word.

Activity:

Find an unfamiliar word in the text and use context clues to determine its meaning before looking it up.

Excellent Work!

You've completed all 11 pages of Lesson 137!

Page 19

Great Job!

You've completed Lesson 137! Come back tomorrow to continue reading The Railway Children.

Reading Comprehension

Answer these questions about today's chapter:

Question 1:

What was the name of the animal that Dr. Dolittle wanted to visit in the Land of the Monkeys?

0 / 20 words

Question 2:

How did Dr. Dolittle plan to travel to the Land of the Monkeys?

0 / 20 words

Question 3:

Do you think Dr. Dolittle was wise to want to visit the Land of the Monkeys? Why or why not?

0 / 20 words

Page 10

Journal Reflection

[Personal reflection prompt related to Chapter 18]

Page 11

Informational Text

Talking to Animals: The Power of Communication

Part 1 of 2

In the world of Dr. Dolittle, the ability to communicate with animals is a remarkable gift. As the story unfolds, we discover that understanding the language of our furry, feathered, and scaly friends is not only fascinating but also crucial to solving problems and embarking on exciting adventures.

Page 12

Talking to Animals: The Power of Communication

Part 2 of 2

Through Dr. Dolittle's experiences, we learn that animals have complex thoughts, feelings, and means of communication. By learning to listen and interpret their unique forms of expression, we can forge deep connections and uncover the secrets of the natural world.

Page 13

Question 1 of 3

1. How does Dr. Dolittle's ability to communicate with animals help him in his adventures?

0 / 15 words

Page 14

Question 2 of 3

2. What are some examples of how animals communicate in the story?

0 / 15 words

Page 15

Question 3 of 3

3. Why is the power of communication between humans and animals important in the context of the story?

0 / 15 words

Page 16

Grammar Workshop

Compound Sentences

What is it?
Compound sentences are made up of two independent clauses joined by a coordinating conjunction (and, but, or, nor, for, so, yet).

Example from today's reading:
"In the story, Dr. Dolittle could talk to the animals, and they could understand him in return."

Your Turn:

Write three compound sentences about today's chapter using different coordinating conjunctions.

Page 17

Narrative Writing

Prompt:

Imagine you are Aunt Em in the cellar. Write about what you're thinking as the storm passes overhead.

Remember: Write a short story or scene with a clear beginning, middle, and end. Include descriptive details and dialogue if appropriate.

Checklist: Did you include a topic sentence? Supporting details? A conclusion?

Page 18

Language Skills

Root Words and Affixes

Learn:
Many words are built from roots (base words) with prefixes (before) and suffixes (after) added.

Activity:

Break down vocabulary words into their parts: prefix + root + suffix. Example: uncomfortable = un + comfort + able

Excellent Work!

Page 9

Reading Comprehension

Answer these questions about today's chapter:

Question 1:

What did the animals help Doctor Dolittle do when he was getting ready to sail to Africa?

0 / 20 words

Question 2:

Why do you think the animals wanted to go with Doctor Dolittle to Africa?

0 / 20 words

Question 3:

What do you think will happen when Doctor Dolittle and the animals arrive in Africa?

0 / 20 words

Page 10

Journal Reflection

[Personal reflection prompt related to Chapter 19]

Page 11

Informational Text

The Amazing Language of Animals

Part 1 of 2

Did you know that animals have their own way of communicating? Just like humans use words, animals use different sounds, body language, and even scents to talk to each other. For example, wolves howl to let their pack know where they are. Bees do a special dance to tell other bees where to find the best flowers. Even though animals can't speak our language, they have very clever ways of getting their message across!

Page 12

The Amazing Language of Animals

Part 2 of 2

In the story of Dr. Dolittle, the main character can understand and speak the languages of all the animals. This allows him to go on exciting adventures, helping sick animals and learning about their incredible abilities. Imagine if you could talk to the animals around you - what kinds of stories do you think they would have to share?

Page 13

Question 1 of 3

1. How do wolves communicate with their pack?

0 / 15 words

Page 14

Question 2 of 3

2. What kind of dance do bees do to tell other bees about flowers?

0 / 15 words

Page 15

Question 3 of 3

3. Why is Dr. Dolittle's ability to speak with animals important in the story?

0 / 15 words

Page 16

Grammar Workshop

Action vs. Linking Verbs

What is it?
A compound sentence has two independent clauses joined by a conjunction like 'and', 'but', or 'or'.

Example from today's reading:
"In the story, Dr. Dolittle can speak with animals, and he uses this ability to go on amazing adventures."

Your Turn:

Find five verbs in today's reading and label each as action or linking.

Page 17

Opinion Writing

Prompt:

Should Dorothy have tried harder to get into the cellar, or was it okay that she stopped to get Toto? Defend your opinion.

Remember: Write a paragraph stating your opinion with at least 2-3 reasons supporting your view. Include a topic sentence and conclusion.

Checklist: Did you include a topic sentence? Supporting details? A conclusion?

Page 18

Language Skills

Idioms and Figurative Language

Learn:
Idioms are phrases that don't mean exactly what the words say (like 'raining cats and dogs').

Activity:

Identify any idioms or figurative language in today's chapter and explain what they really mean.

Excellent Work!

You've completed all 11 pages of Lesson 139!

Page 19

Great Job!

You've completed Lesson 139! Come back tomorrow to continue reading The Railway Children.

Lesson 140

Assessment Day

The Railway Children

Assessment Instructions

Today is an assessment day! You'll be tested on:

  • Vocabulary: 10 words from Days 136-140

  • Grammar: Adjectives and Adjective Order

Time needed: About 45-60 minutes

2. humble

Welcome to Lesson 141!

  • Read informational text: "Related Non-Fiction"

Reading: Pippi Longstocking

Book: A Little Princess by Frances Hodgson Burnett

Page 5

Page 2 of 5

📚 Placeholder for Pippi Longstocking - Chapter 21, Part 2

This page will contain approximately 500 words from the chapter when book text is integrated. The formatting is ready: 11px font, line-height 1.2, ~5 pages per chapter.

Page 6

Page 3 of 5

📚 Placeholder for Pippi Longstocking - Chapter 21, Part 3

This page will contain approximately 500 words from the chapter when book text is integrated. The formatting is ready: 11px font, line-height 1.2, ~5 pages per chapter.

Page 7

Page 4 of 5

📚 Placeholder for Pippi Longstocking - Chapter 21, Part 4

This page will contain approximately 500 words from the chapter when book text is integrated. The formatting is ready: 11px font, line-height 1.2, ~5 pages per chapter.

Page 8

Page 5 of 5

📚 Placeholder for Pippi Longstocking - Chapter 21, Part 5

This page will contain approximately 500 words from the chapter when book text is integrated. The formatting is ready: 11px font, line-height 1.2, ~5 pages per chapter.

Page 9

Reading Comprehension

Answer these questions about today's chapter:

Question 1:

What did Doctor Dolittle discover about the monkeys' language that surprised him?

0 / 20 words

Question 2:

Why do you think the monkeys were so eager to learn English from Doctor Dolittle?

0 / 20 words

Question 3:

Do you think Doctor Dolittle was right to teach the monkeys English? Explain your reasoning.

0 / 20 words

Page 10

Journal Reflection

[Personal reflection prompt related to Chapter 21]

Page 11

Informational Text

The Amazing World of Animal Communication

Part 1 of 2

In the story of Dr. Dolittle, we learn about the incredible ability of animals to communicate with each other and with humans. Just like people, animals use a variety of methods to share information, express their needs, and even tell stories. From the chatter of birds to the intricate dances of bees, the natural world is full of fascinating forms of communication.

Page 12

The Amazing World of Animal Communication

Part 2 of 2

As Dr. Dolittle discovers, the key to understanding animal communication is being open-minded and patient. By learning the languages of different creatures, he is able to embark on incredible adventures and help those in need. This reminds us that when we take the time to listen and observe, we can uncover the wonders of the animal kingdom all around us.

Page 13

Question 1 of 3

1. How do animals communicate with each other in the story of Dr. Dolittle?

0 / 15 words

Page 14

Question 2 of 3

2. What are some examples of different types of animal communication mentioned in the article?

0 / 15 words

Page 15

Question 3 of 3

3. Why is it important for Dr. Dolittle to learn the languages of the animals he encounters?

0 / 15 words

Page 16

Grammar Workshop

Past Progressive Tense

What is it?
Adjectives are words that describe nouns, like the animals in the story. Using vivid adjectives can help us paint a clearer picture of the different creatures Dr. Dolittle encounters.

Example from today's reading:
"In the book, the parrot is described as 'brilliant' and the dog as 'loyal'. What other adjectives could we use to describe the animals in the story?"

Your Turn:

Write three sentences using the past progressive tense to describe things happening in today's chapter.

Page 17

Narrative Writing

Prompt:

Imagine you are Aunt Em in the cellar. Write about what you're thinking as the storm passes overhead.

Remember: Write a short story or scene with a clear beginning, middle, and end. Include descriptive details and dialogue if appropriate.

Checklist: Did you include a topic sentence? Supporting details? A conclusion?

Page 18

Language Skills

Similes and Metaphors

Learn:
A simile compares two things using 'like' or 'as'. A metaphor says one thing IS another thing.

Activity:

Find the simile in today's reading or create your own simile about the story.

Excellent Work!

You've completed all 11 pages of Lesson 141!

Page 19

Great Job!

You've completed Lesson 141! Come back tomorrow to continue reading The Railway Children.

Reading: Pippi Longstocking

Page 5 of 5

Page 9

Reading Comprehension

Answer these questions about today's chapter:

Question 1:

What kind of animals did the Doctor take with him on his trip to Africa?

0 / 20 words

Question 2:

Why did the Doctor decide to go to Africa?

0 / 20 words

Question 3:

Do you think the Doctor made the right decision to go to Africa? Why or why not?

0 / 20 words

Page 10

Journal Reflection

[Personal reflection prompt related to Chapter 22]

Page 11

Informational Text

Animal Doctors: Helping Creatures Communicate

Part 1 of 2

Have you ever wondered how animals communicate with each other? In the story of Dr. Dolittle, the doctor has a special ability to talk to animals and understand their needs. This is an important skill for any animal doctor to have. By learning how to communicate with different creatures, Dr. Dolittle can help them feel better and stay healthy.

Page 12

Animal Doctors: Helping Creatures Communicate

Part 2 of 2

Communication is key in the world of animal care. Doctors need to be able to listen to the animals and figure out what is wrong. They also have to explain procedures and treatments in a way that the animals can understand. With patience and practice, animal doctors like Dr. Dolittle can become true masters of interspecies communication.

Page 13

Question 1 of 3

1. How does Dr. Dolittle's ability to talk to animals help him in his work?

0 / 15 words

Page 14

Question 2 of 3

2. Why is communication important for animal doctors?

0 / 15 words

Page 15

Question 3 of 3

3. What skills do you think an animal doctor needs to have?

0 / 15 words

Page 16

Grammar Workshop

Prepositional Phrases

What is it?
Proper nouns are the names of specific people, places, or things. They should always be capitalized.

Example from today's reading:
"In the story, 'Dr. Dolittle' and 'Hugh Lofting' are both proper nouns."

Your Turn:

Find three prepositional phrases in today's reading and circle the preposition in each one.

Page 17

Opinion Writing

Prompt:

Should Dorothy have tried harder to get into the cellar, or was it okay that she stopped to get Toto? Defend your opinion.

Remember: Write a paragraph stating your opinion with at least 2-3 reasons supporting your view. Include a topic sentence and conclusion.

Checklist: Did you include a topic sentence? Supporting details? A conclusion?

Page 18

Language Skills

Context Clues

Learn:
Use surrounding words to figure out the meaning of an unfamiliar word.

Activity:

Find an unfamiliar word in the text and use context clues to determine its meaning before looking it up.

Excellent Work!

You've completed all 11 pages of Lesson 142!

Page 19

Great Job!

You've completed Lesson 142! Come back tomorrow to continue reading The Railway Children.

3rd Grade ELA

The Railway Children

  • Read from A Little Princess
  • Learn 2 new vocabulary words
  • Read informational text: "Related Non-Fiction"
  • Practice grammar: Compound Sentences
  • Write: Informative Writing
  • Explore: Root Words and Affixes

Page 4 of 5

📚 Placeholder for Pippi Longstocking - Chapter 23, Part 4

This page will contain approximately 500 words from the chapter when book text is integrated. The formatting is ready: 11px font, line-height 1.2, ~5 pages per chapter.

Page 8

Page 5 of 5

📚 Placeholder for Pippi Longstocking - Chapter 23, Part 5

This page will contain approximately 500 words from the chapter when book text is integrated. The formatting is ready: 11px font, line-height 1.2, ~5 pages per chapter.

Page 9

Reading Comprehension

Answer these questions about today's chapter:

Question 1:

What did Dr. Dolittle do to prepare for his journey across the sea?

0 / 20 words

Question 2:

Why did the animals want to go with Dr. Dolittle on his journey?

0 / 20 words

Question 3:

Do you think it was a good idea for the animals to join Dr. Dolittle on his journey? Why or why not?

0 / 20 words

Page 10

Journal Reflection

[Personal reflection prompt related to Chapter 23]

Page 11

Informational Text

Animal Doctors and Their Patients

Part 1 of 2

Have you ever wondered what it would be like to be an animal doctor? In the story of Dr. Dolittle, we learn that he was able to talk to animals and understand their needs. This allowed him to provide the best care for his animal patients. Imagine being able to ask a dog or a bird how they are feeling and what is wrong with them.

Page 12

Animal Doctors and Their Patients

Part 2 of 2

Being an animal doctor requires a special set of skills. You need to be able to communicate with the animals, understand their behavior, and know how to treat their unique medical needs. Dr. Dolittle's ability to talk to animals made him a very successful doctor. He was able to help many sick and injured animals because he could understand what they were telling him.

Page 13

Question 1 of 3

1. What special skill did Dr. Dolittle have that helped him care for his animal patients?

0 / 15 words

Page 14

Question 2 of 3

2. Why do you think being able to communicate with animals is important for an animal doctor?

0 / 15 words

Page 15

Question 3 of 3

3. What kinds of challenges do you think an animal doctor might face that are different from a doctor who treats humans?

0 / 15 words

Page 16

Grammar Workshop

Compound Sentences

What is it?
Pronouns are words that replace nouns to avoid repetition. They can be used to refer to people, places, or things.

Example from today's reading:
"In the story, Dr. Dolittle uses 'he' and 'him' to refer to the animals he is talking to."

Your Turn:

Write three compound sentences about today's chapter using different coordinating conjunctions.

Page 17

Informative Writing

Prompt:

Describe what life was like for Dorothy in Kansas before the cyclone. Include at least 3 specific details.

Remember: Write a paragraph explaining something from the story. Include clear details and examples.

Checklist: Did you include a topic sentence? Supporting details? A conclusion?

Page 18

Language Skills

Root Words and Affixes

Learn:
Many words are built from roots (base words) with prefixes (before) and suffixes (after) added.

Activity:

Break down vocabulary words into their parts: prefix + root + suffix. Example: uncomfortable = un + comfort + able

Excellent Work!

You've completed all 11 pages of Lesson 143!

Page 19

Great Job!

You've completed Lesson 143! Come back tomorrow to continue reading The Railway Children.

Reading Comprehension

Answer these questions about today's chapter:

Question 1:

What did the animals do to help Dr. Dolittle escape from the pirates?

0 / 20 words

Question 2:

Why do you think the animals decided to help Dr. Dolittle escape?

0 / 20 words

Question 3:

Do you think Dr. Dolittle made the right decision to leave the island and go back home? Why or why not?

0 / 20 words

Page 10

Journal Reflection

[Personal reflection prompt related to Chapter 24]

Page 11

Informational Text

How Animals Communicate in the Jungle

Part 1 of 2

In the jungle, animals have developed amazing ways to communicate with each other. From the loud roars of lions to the intricate dances of bees, animals use a variety of sounds, movements, and even scents to share information and interact. This is especially important for animals like Dr. Dolittle's animal friends, who need to work together to survive in the wild.

Page 12

How Animals Communicate in the Jungle

Part 2 of 2

Some animals, like parrots and dolphins, can even learn to communicate with humans using sounds and gestures. This allows them to express their needs and feelings, and even form close bonds with the people they interact with. By understanding how animals communicate, we can better appreciate the rich and complex lives of our fellow creatures.

Page 13

Question 1 of 3

1. How do animals in the jungle communicate with each other?

0 / 15 words

Page 14

Question 2 of 3

2. Why is communication important for animals like Dr. Dolittle's friends?

0 / 15 words

Page 15

Question 3 of 3

3. What are some examples of animals that can communicate with humans?

0 / 15 words

Page 16

Grammar Workshop

Action vs. Linking Verbs

What is it?
Proper nouns are the specific names of people, places, or things. They should be capitalized in writing.

Example from today's reading:
"In the story, the name 'Dr. Dolittle' is a proper noun that refers to the main character."

Your Turn:

Find five verbs in today's reading and label each as action or linking.

Page 17

Narrative Writing

Prompt:

Imagine you are Aunt Em in the cellar. Write about what you're thinking as the storm passes overhead.

Remember: Write a short story or scene with a clear beginning, middle, and end. Include descriptive details and dialogue if appropriate.

Checklist: Did you include a topic sentence? Supporting details? A conclusion?

Page 18

Language Skills

Idioms and Figurative Language

Learn:
Idioms are phrases that don't mean exactly what the words say (like 'raining cats and dogs').

Activity:

Identify any idioms or figurative language in today's chapter and explain what they really mean.

Excellent Work!

Page 9

Reading Comprehension

Answer these questions about today's chapter:

Question 1:

What animal did Doctor Dolittle take with him on his voyage to Africa?

0 / 20 words

Question 2:

Why do you think Doctor Dolittle decided to travel to Africa?

0 / 20 words

Question 3:

How do you think Doctor Dolittle's animal friends felt about him leaving them behind?

0 / 20 words

Page 10

Journal Reflection

[Personal reflection prompt related to Chapter 26]

Page 11

Informational Text

The Amazing World of Animal Communication

Part 1 of 2

Have you ever wondered how animals communicate with each other? In the story of Dr. Dolittle, we learn about the incredible ability of animals to talk and understand human language. Just like humans, animals use a variety of methods to communicate, from body language to sounds and even scents. By observing the different ways animals interact, we can gain a deeper appreciation for the complex and fascinating world of animal communication.

Page 12

The Amazing World of Animal Communication

Part 2 of 2

As we follow Dr. Dolittle on his adventures, we see how he is able to connect with the animal kingdom in a way that few humans can. By learning their languages, he is able to understand their needs, concerns, and even their stories. This allows him to help the animals in ways that no one else can. Through this story, we can learn about the importance of understanding and respecting the natural world around us.

Page 13

Question 1 of 3

1. How do animals communicate with each other?

0 / 15 words

Page 14

Question 2 of 3

2. What makes Dr. Dolittle unique in his ability to communicate with animals?

0 / 15 words

Page 15

Question 3 of 3

3. Why is it important to understand animal communication?

0 / 15 words

Page 16

Grammar Workshop

Past Progressive Tense

What is it?
Conjunctions are words that connect two sentences or ideas together. They help to show the relationship between the different parts of a sentence.

Example from today's reading:
"In the story, Dr. Dolittle says, 'I can speak to the animals, and they can speak to me.' The conjunction 'and' connects the two ideas together."

Your Turn:

Write three sentences using the past progressive tense to describe things happening in today's chapter.

Page 17

Informative Writing

Prompt:

Describe what life was like for Dorothy in Kansas before the cyclone. Include at least 3 specific details.

Remember: Write a paragraph explaining something from the story. Include clear details and examples.

Checklist: Did you include a topic sentence? Supporting details? A conclusion?

Page 18

Language Skills

Similes and Metaphors

Learn:
A simile compares two things using 'like' or 'as'. A metaphor says one thing IS another thing.

Activity:

Find the simile in today's reading or create your own simile about the story.

Excellent Work!

Book: A Little Princess by Frances Hodgson Burnett

Page 5

Page 2 of 5

📚 Placeholder for Pippi Longstocking - Chapter 27, Part 2

This page will contain approximately 500 words from the chapter when book text is integrated. The formatting is ready: 11px font, line-height 1.2, ~5 pages per chapter.

Page 6

Page 3 of 5

📚 Placeholder for Pippi Longstocking - Chapter 27, Part 3

This page will contain approximately 500 words from the chapter when book text is integrated. The formatting is ready: 11px font, line-height 1.2, ~5 pages per chapter.

Page 7

Page 4 of 5

📚 Placeholder for Pippi Longstocking - Chapter 27, Part 4

This page will contain approximately 500 words from the chapter when book text is integrated. The formatting is ready: 11px font, line-height 1.2, ~5 pages per chapter.

Page 8

Page 5 of 5

📚 Placeholder for Pippi Longstocking - Chapter 27, Part 5

This page will contain approximately 500 words from the chapter when book text is integrated. The formatting is ready: 11px font, line-height 1.2, ~5 pages per chapter.

Page 9

Reading Comprehension

Answer these questions about today's chapter:

Question 1:

What was the name of the parrot that Dr. Dolittle talked to?

0 / 20 words

Question 2:

Why did the parrot want to go back to his home in Africa?

0 / 20 words

Question 3:

Do you think it was a good idea for Dr. Dolittle to take the parrot back to Africa? Why or why not?

0 / 20 words

Page 10

Journal Reflection

[Personal reflection prompt related to Chapter 27]

Page 11

Informational Text

The Amazing World of Animal Communication

Part 1 of 2

Have you ever wondered how animals talk to each other? In the story of Dr. Dolittle, the doctor has the amazing ability to understand and speak with all kinds of animals. This is called animal communication, and it's a fascinating topic. Animals use a variety of methods to communicate, such as body language, sounds, and even scents.

Page 12

The Amazing World of Animal Communication

Part 2 of 2

Did you know that whales can communicate with each other over vast distances in the ocean? Or that ants leave chemical trails to guide their friends to food? Animals have developed incredible ways to share information and work together. In the adventures of Dr. Dolittle, we get a glimpse into this amazing world of animal communication.

Page 13

Question 1 of 3

1. What are some ways that animals communicate with each other?

0 / 15 words

Page 14

Question 2 of 3

2. How does Dr. Dolittle's ability to speak with animals help him in the story?

0 / 15 words

Page 15

Question 3 of 3

3. Why is animal communication important for the animals' survival and well-being?

0 / 15 words

Page 16

Grammar Workshop

Prepositional Phrases

What is it?
A compound sentence is made up of two or more independent clauses joined by a conjunction, such as 'and', 'but', or 'or'.

Example from today's reading:
"In the story, Dr. Dolittle can speak with the animals, and they help him on his adventures."

Your Turn:

Find three prepositional phrases in today's reading and circle the preposition in each one.

Page 17

Narrative Writing

Prompt:

Imagine you are Aunt Em in the cellar. Write about what you're thinking as the storm passes overhead.

Remember: Write a short story or scene with a clear beginning, middle, and end. Include descriptive details and dialogue if appropriate.

Checklist: Did you include a topic sentence? Supporting details? A conclusion?

Page 18

Language Skills

Context Clues

Learn:
Use surrounding words to figure out the meaning of an unfamiliar word.

Activity:

Find an unfamiliar word in the text and use context clues to determine its meaning before looking it up.

Excellent Work!

You've completed all 11 pages of Lesson 147!

Page 19

Great Job!

You've completed Lesson 147! Come back tomorrow to continue reading The Railway Children.

Lesson 148

3rd Grade ELA

The Railway Children

Chapter 28

Welcome to Lesson 148!

Today you will:

  • Read from A Little Princess
  • Learn 2 new vocabulary words
  • Read informational text: "Related Non-Fiction"
  • Practice grammar: Compound Sentences
  • Write: Opinion Writing
  • Explore: Root Words and Affixes

Time needed: About 45-60 minutes

Reading: Pippi Longstocking

Chapter 28

Page 3 of 5

📚 Placeholder for Pippi Longstocking - Chapter 28, Part 3

Page 4 of 5

📚 Placeholder for Pippi Longstocking - Chapter 28, Part 4

This page will contain approximately 500 words from the chapter when book text is integrated. The formatting is ready: 11px font, line-height 1.2, ~5 pages per chapter.

Page 8

Page 5 of 5

📚 Placeholder for Pippi Longstocking - Chapter 28, Part 5

This page will contain approximately 500 words from the chapter when book text is integrated. The formatting is ready: 11px font, line-height 1.2, ~5 pages per chapter.

Page 9

Reading Comprehension

Answer these questions about today's chapter:

Question 1:

What was the name of the parrot that Dr. Dolittle taught to talk?

0 / 20 words

Question 2:

Why do you think the parrot decided to go back to the ship with Dr. Dolittle?

0 / 20 words

Question 3:

Do you think Dr. Dolittle made the right decision to take the parrot back to the ship? Why or why not?

0 / 20 words

Page 10

Journal Reflection

[Personal reflection prompt related to Chapter 28]

Page 11

Informational Text

Exploring the Animal Kingdom with Dr. Dolittle

Part 1 of 2

In Hugh Lofting's classic story, "The Story of Dr. Dolittle," readers are introduced to a remarkable doctor who has the ability to communicate with animals. As Dr. Dolittle embarks on an adventure to the African continent, he encounters a wide variety of fascinating creatures and learns about their unique languages and behaviors.

Page 12

Exploring the Animal Kingdom with Dr. Dolittle

Part 2 of 2

Through his interactions with the animals, Dr. Dolittle not only helps them but also gains a deeper understanding of the natural world. This story encourages readers to appreciate the diversity of the animal kingdom and the importance of effective communication, both with other humans and with the creatures that share our planet.

Page 13

Question 1 of 3

1. How does Dr. Dolittle's ability to communicate with animals help him during his adventure?

0 / 15 words

Page 14

Question 2 of 3

2. What are some of the unique characteristics of the animals that Dr. Dolittle encounters?

0 / 15 words

Page 15

Question 3 of 3

3. Why is it important for humans to understand and respect the communication methods of animals?

0 / 15 words

Page 16

Grammar Workshop

Compound Sentences

What is it?
A compound sentence is formed when two independent clauses are joined together using a coordinating conjunction (e.g., and, but, or, nor, for, so, yet).

Example from today's reading:
"In the book, Dr. Dolittle could speak with the animals, and they could understand him in return."

Your Turn:

Write three compound sentences about today's chapter using different coordinating conjunctions.

Page 17

Opinion Writing

Prompt:

Should Dorothy have tried harder to get into the cellar, or was it okay that she stopped to get Toto? Defend your opinion.

Remember: Write a paragraph stating your opinion with at least 2-3 reasons supporting your view. Include a topic sentence and conclusion.

Checklist: Did you include a topic sentence? Supporting details? A conclusion?

Page 18

Language Skills

Root Words and Affixes

Learn:
Many words are built from roots (base words) with prefixes (before) and suffixes (after) added.

Activity:

Break down vocabulary words into their parts: prefix + root + suffix. Example: uncomfortable = un + comfort + able

Excellent Work!

You've completed all 11 pages of Lesson 148!

Page 19

Great Job!

You've completed Lesson 148! Come back tomorrow to continue reading The Railway Children.

Reading: Pippi Longstocking

Page 5 of 5

This page will contain approximately 500 words from the chapter when book text is integrated. The formatting is ready: 11px font, line-height 1.2, ~5 pages per chapter.

Page 9

Reading Comprehension

Answer these questions about today's chapter:

Question 1:

What did the parrot teach Dr. Dolittle to do?

0 / 20 words

Question 2:

Why did the parrot want to learn how to talk to people?

0 / 20 words

Question 3:

Do you think it was a good idea for Dr. Dolittle to teach the parrot to talk? Why or why not?

0 / 20 words

Page 10

Journal Reflection

[Personal reflection prompt related to Chapter 29]

Page 11

Informational Text

The Amazing World of Animal Communication

Part 1 of 2

Did you know that animals communicate in all sorts of fascinating ways? From the chattering of birds to the howling of wolves, animals use a variety of sounds, body language, and even scents to express themselves. In the story of Dr. Dolittle, we learn about the doctor's ability to understand and talk to animals. This adventure shows us how important communication is, not just for humans, but for all the creatures of the world.

Page 12

The Amazing World of Animal Communication

Part 2 of 2

Animals communicate for many reasons, such as to warn others of danger, to find mates, or to mark their territory. The animals in Dr. Dolittle's story use their unique forms of communication to help him on his adventures. By understanding how animals talk, Dr. Dolittle is able to learn valuable information and work together with the creatures he meets. Communication is the key to building relationships and overcoming challenges, both for people and for the animal kingdom.

Page 13

Question 1 of 3

1. What are some of the ways that animals communicate with each other?

0 / 15 words

Page 14

Question 2 of 3

2. How does Dr. Dolittle's ability to talk to animals help him on his adventures?

0 / 15 words

Page 15

Question 3 of 3

3. Why is communication important for both humans and animals?

0 / 15 words

Page 16

Grammar Workshop

Action vs. Linking Verbs

What is it?
Adjectives are words that describe nouns. They can tell us more about the size, color, or other qualities of a person, place, or thing.

Example from today's reading:
"In the story, Dr. Dolittle meets many fascinating animals, such as the wise old parrot and the brave little mouse."

Your Turn:

Find five verbs in today's reading and label each as action or linking.

Page 17

Informative Writing

Prompt:

Describe what life was like for Dorothy in Kansas before the cyclone. Include at least 3 specific details.

Remember: Write a paragraph explaining something from the story. Include clear details and examples.

Checklist: Did you include a topic sentence? Supporting details? A conclusion?

Page 18

Language Skills

Idioms and Figurative Language

Learn:
Idioms are phrases that don't mean exactly what the words say (like 'raining cats and dogs').

Activity:

Identify any idioms or figurative language in today's chapter and explain what they really mean.

Excellent Work!

You've completed all 11 pages of Lesson 149!

Page 19

Great Job!

You've completed Lesson 149! Come back tomorrow to continue reading The Railway Children.

Day 150: Day 150 Assessment - 3rd Grade BedrockELA

Page 1

Lesson 150

Assessment Day

The Railway Children

Page 2

Assessment Instructions

Today is an assessment day! You'll be tested on:

  • Vocabulary: 10 words from Days 146-150
  • Reading Comprehension: Questions about the chapters
  • Grammar: Adjectives and Adjective Order
  • Writing: Narrative Writing

Time needed: About 45-60 minutes

Tip: Take your time and do your best work!

Page 3

Vocabulary Assessment

Write the definition for each word. Use complete sentences.

1. delightful

2. humble

3. radiant

4. miracle

5. terrific

6. solemn

7. earnest

8. whisper

9. struggle

10. comfort

Page 4

Reading Comprehension Test

Answer these questions about the chapters you read this week:

Question 1:

[Comprehension question about this week's chapters]

Question 2:

[Comprehension question about this week's chapters]

Question 3:

[Comprehension question about this week's chapters]

Question 4:

[Comprehension question about this week's chapters]

Question 5:

[Comprehension question about this week's chapters]

Page 5

Grammar Assessment

Adjectives and Adjective Order

Write three sentences with multiple adjectives describing characters or places from the chapter.

Page 6

Writing Assessment

Narrative Writing

Prompt:

Imagine you are Aunt Em in the cellar. Write about what you're thinking as the storm passes overhead.

Requirements: Write a short story or scene with a clear beginning, middle, and end. Include descriptive details and dialogue if appropriate.

Page 7

Assessment Complete!

Great work on your assessment! You've completed 5 full days of The Railway Children.

See you on Day 151!

Page 5 of 5

📚 Placeholder for The Secret Garden - Chapter 1, Part 5

This page will contain approximately 500 words from the chapter when book text is integrated. The formatting is ready: 11px font, line-height 1.2, ~5 pages per chapter.

Page 9

Reading Comprehension

Answer these questions about today's chapter:

Question 1:

What task did Dr. Dolittle ask the animals to help him with?

0 / 20 words

Question 2:

Why do you think the animals were hesitant to help Dr. Dolittle at first?

0 / 20 words

Question 3:

Do you think the animals made the right decision to help Dr. Dolittle in the end? Explain your reasoning.

0 / 20 words

Page 10

Journal Reflection

[Personal reflection prompt related to Chapter 1]

Page 11

Informational Text

The Wonders of the Animal Kingdom: Communication and Adventure in the World of Dr. Dolittle

Part 1 of 2

In the enchanting world of Dr. Dolittle, we discover the incredible abilities of animals to communicate with humans. Through his unique gift, the good doctor uncovers the rich inner lives of the creatures around him, revealing the depth of their emotions, intelligence, and unique perspectives on the world.

Page 12

The Wonders of the Animal Kingdom: Communication and Adventure in the World of Dr. Dolittle

Part 2 of 2

Join Dr. Dolittle on his thrilling adventures as he navigates the challenges and wonders of the animal kingdom. From rescuing a trapped seal to deciphering the languages of exotic birds, his stories inspire us to appreciate the remarkable diversity and complexity of the natural world.

Page 13

Question 1 of 3

1. How does Dr. Dolittle's ability to communicate with animals shape his experiences and relationships?

0 / 15 words

Page 14

Question 2 of 3

2. What are some of the unique ways animals are able to express themselves in the story?

0 / 15 words

Page 15

Question 3 of 3

3. What lessons about the natural world and our place in it can we learn from Dr. Dolittle's adventures?

0 / 15 words

Page 16

Grammar Workshop

Past Progressive Tense

What is it?
Compound sentences are made up of two independent clauses connected by a coordinating conjunction (such as 'and', 'but', or 'or'). This allows the writer to combine related ideas and create more complex and interesting sentences.

Example from today's reading:
"Dr. Dolittle could speak to animals, and they understood him perfectly."

Your Turn:

Write three sentences using the past progressive tense to describe things happening in today's chapter.

Page 17

Opinion Writing

Prompt:

Should Dorothy have tried harder to get into the cellar, or was it okay that she stopped to get Toto? Defend your opinion.

Remember: Write a paragraph stating your opinion with at least 2-3 reasons supporting your view. Include a topic sentence and conclusion.

Checklist: Did you include a topic sentence? Supporting details? A conclusion?

Page 18

Language Skills

Similes and Metaphors

Learn:
A simile compares two things using 'like' or 'as'. A metaphor says one thing IS another thing.

Activity:

Find the simile in today's reading or create your own simile about the story.

Excellent Work!

You've completed all 11 pages of Lesson 151!

Page 19

Great Job!

You've completed Lesson 151! Come back tomorrow to continue reading Heidi.

Page 9

Reading Comprehension

Answer these questions about today's chapter:

Question 1:

What did the animals do to help Dr. Dolittle reach the Land of the Monkeys?

0 / 20 words

Question 2:

Why do you think the monkeys were so excited to see Dr. Dolittle?

0 / 20 words

Question 3:

Do you think Dr. Dolittle should have tried to learn the monkey language instead of asking them to learn English? Why or why not?

0 / 20 words

Page 10

Journal Reflection

[Personal reflection prompt related to Chapter 2]

Page 11

Informational Text

The Amazing Abilities of Animals

Part 1 of 2

Did you know that animals can communicate in incredible ways? From the intricate dances of honeybees to the complex calls of whales, animals have developed sophisticated methods of sharing information. In the story of Dr. Dolittle, the doctor is able to understand and speak with all kinds of animals, allowing him to go on extraordinary adventures.

Page 12

The Amazing Abilities of Animals

Part 2 of 2

Animals use a variety of sounds, gestures, and even scents to communicate. For example, elephants can recognize the voices of their herd members and use their trunks to greet one another. Dolphins click and whistle to navigate the ocean and find food. Exploring the amazing communication abilities of animals can lead to incredible discoveries and adventures, just like in the story of Dr. Dolittle.

Page 13

Question 1 of 3

1. How do animals communicate with each other?

0 / 15 words

Page 14

Question 2 of 3

2. What are some examples of animal communication mentioned in the story?

0 / 15 words

Page 15

Question 3 of 3

3. Why is understanding animal communication important for going on adventures?

0 / 15 words

Page 16

Grammar Workshop

Prepositional Phrases

What is it?
Adverbs are words that describe how an action is done. They often end in -ly, such as 'quickly' or 'quietly'.

Example from today's reading:
"In the story, Dr. Dolittle speaks gently to the animals to gain their trust."

Your Turn:

Find three prepositional phrases in today's reading and circle the preposition in each one.

Page 17

Informative Writing

Prompt:

Describe what life was like for Dorothy in Kansas before the cyclone. Include at least 3 specific details.

Remember: Write a paragraph explaining something from the story. Include clear details and examples.

Checklist: Did you include a topic sentence? Supporting details? A conclusion?

Page 18

Language Skills

Context Clues

Learn:
Use surrounding words to figure out the meaning of an unfamiliar word.

Activity:

Find an unfamiliar word in the text and use context clues to determine its meaning before looking it up.

Excellent Work!

Page 9

Reading Comprehension

Answer these questions about today's chapter:

Question 1:

What special power did Dr. Dolittle have that allowed him to talk to animals?

0 / 20 words

Question 2:

Why did the animals want to travel with Dr. Dolittle to the land of green meadows?

0 / 20 words

Question 3:

Do you think it was a good idea for the animals to follow Dr. Dolittle on his journey? Why or why not?

0 / 20 words

Page 10

Journal Reflection

[Personal reflection prompt related to Chapter 3]

Page 11

Informational Text

The Wonders of Animal Communication

Part 1 of 2

In the world of Dr. Dolittle, animals can talk to each other and even to humans! This incredible ability allows them to share their thoughts, feelings, and experiences. From the chatty parrots to the curious dogs, the animals in this story demonstrate the power of communication.

Page 12

The Wonders of Animal Communication

Part 2 of 2

By learning to understand animal language, Dr. Dolittle embarks on incredible adventures, helping the creatures in need. Their ability to communicate opens up new perspectives and helps solve problems in creative ways. This story highlights the importance of listening and connecting with the natural world around us.

Page 13

Question 1 of 3

1. What are some examples of animals communicating in the story?

0 / 15 words

Page 14

Question 2 of 3

2. How does Dr. Dolittle's ability to understand animal language help him on his adventures?

0 / 15 words

Page 15

Question 3 of 3

3. Why is the theme of communication important in this story?

0 / 15 words

Page 16

Grammar Workshop

Compound Sentences

What is it?
Adjectives are words that describe nouns, helping to provide more detail and information.

Example from today's reading:
"In the story, the author uses adjectives like 'chatty' and 'curious' to describe the animals and their communication."

Your Turn:

Write three compound sentences about today's chapter using different coordinating conjunctions.

Page 17

Narrative Writing

Prompt:

Imagine you are Aunt Em in the cellar. Write about what you're thinking as the storm passes overhead.

Remember: Write a short story or scene with a clear beginning, middle, and end. Include descriptive details and dialogue if appropriate.

Checklist: Did you include a topic sentence? Supporting details? A conclusion?

Page 18

Language Skills

Root Words and Affixes

Learn:
Many words are built from roots (base words) with prefixes (before) and suffixes (after) added.

Activity:

Break down vocabulary words into their parts: prefix + root + suffix. Example: uncomfortable = un + comfort + able

Day 154: Lesson 154 - 3rd Grade BedrockELA

Page 1

Lesson 154

3rd Grade ELA

Heidi

Chapter 4

Page 2

Welcome to Lesson 154!

Today you will:

  • Read from The Secret Garden
  • Learn 2 new vocabulary words
  • Read informational text: "Related Non-Fiction"
  • Practice grammar: Action vs. Linking Verbs
  • Write: Opinion Writing
  • Explore: Idioms and Figurative Language

Time needed: About 45-60 minutes

Page 3

Vocabulary Words

dialect

Look up this word in a dictionary, then write the definition in your own words:

0 / 10 words
hearty

Look up this word in a dictionary, then write the definition in your own words:

0 / 10 words

Watch for these words in today's reading!

Page 4

Reading: The Secret Garden

Chapter 4

Page 1 of 5

📚 Placeholder for The Secret Garden - Chapter 4, Part 1

This page will contain approximately 500 words from the chapter when book text is integrated. The formatting is ready: 11px font, line-height 1.2, ~5 pages per chapter.

Book: The Secret Garden by Frances Hodgson Burnett

Page 5

Page 2 of 5

📚 Placeholder for The Secret Garden - Chapter 4, Part 2

This page will contain approximately 500 words from the chapter when book text is integrated. The formatting is ready: 11px font, line-height 1.2, ~5 pages per chapter.

Page 6

Page 3 of 5

📚 Placeholder for The Secret Garden - Chapter 4, Part 3

This page will contain approximately 500 words from the chapter when book text is integrated. The formatting is ready: 11px font, line-height 1.2, ~5 pages per chapter.

Page 7

Page 4 of 5

📚 Placeholder for The Secret Garden - Chapter 4, Part 4

This page will contain approximately 500 words from the chapter when book text is integrated. The formatting is ready: 11px font, line-height 1.2, ~5 pages per chapter.

Page 8

Page 5 of 5

📚 Placeholder for The Secret Garden - Chapter 4, Part 5

This page will contain approximately 500 words from the chapter when book text is integrated. The formatting is ready: 11px font, line-height 1.2, ~5 pages per chapter.

Page 9

Reading Comprehension

Answer these questions about today's chapter:

Question 1:

What did the animals ask Dr. Dolittle to do for them?

0 / 20 words

Question 2:

Why do you think the animals wanted Dr. Dolittle to stay and help them?

0 / 20 words

Question 3:

Do you think Dr. Dolittle should have stayed with the animals or continued on his journey? Explain your answer.

0 / 20 words

Page 10

Journal Reflection

[Personal reflection prompt related to Chapter 4]

Page 11

Informational Text

Talking with Animals: How Dr. Dolittle Communicates

Part 1 of 2

Did you know that some people can talk to animals? In the story of Dr. Dolittle, the main character has a special ability to communicate with all kinds of creatures. He can understand what they are saying and they can understand him too. This allows Dr. Dolittle to go on amazing adventures and help the animals in need.

Page 12

Talking with Animals: How Dr. Dolittle Communicates

Part 2 of 2

As Dr. Dolittle travels to different places, he uses his gift of animal communication to solve problems and go on exciting journeys. He talks to dogs, birds, monkeys, and even a talking parrot! Dr. Dolittle's ability to connect with the animal world is a unique and valuable skill that helps him learn about the creatures around him.

Page 13

Question 1 of 3

1. How does Dr. Dolittle communicate with the animals?

0 / 15 words

Page 14

Question 2 of 3

2. What kind of animals can Dr. Dolittle talk to?

0 / 15 words

Page 15

Question 3 of 3

3. Why is Dr. Dolittle's ability to communicate with animals helpful?

0 / 15 words

Page 16

Grammar Workshop

Action vs. Linking Verbs

What is it?
Pronouns are words that take the place of nouns. They help us avoid repeating the same words over and over.

Example from today's reading:
"In the story, Dr. Dolittle says, 'I can talk to the animals and they understand me.'"

Your Turn:

Find five verbs in today's reading and label each as action or linking.

Page 17

Opinion Writing

Prompt:

Should Dorothy have tried harder to get into the cellar, or was it okay that she stopped to get Toto? Defend your opinion.

Remember: Write a paragraph stating your opinion with at least 2-3 reasons supporting your view. Include a topic sentence and conclusion.

Checklist: Did you include a topic sentence? Supporting details? A conclusion?

Page 18

Language Skills

Idioms and Figurative Language

Learn:
Idioms are phrases that don't mean exactly what the words say (like 'raining cats and dogs').

Activity:

Identify any idioms or figurative language in today's chapter and explain what they really mean.

Excellent Work!

You've completed all 11 pages of Lesson 154!

Page 19

Great Job!

You've completed Lesson 154! Come back tomorrow to continue reading Heidi.

Day 155: Day 155 Assessment - 3rd Grade BedrockELA

Page 1

Lesson 155

Assessment Day

Heidi

Page 2

Assessment Instructions

Today is an assessment day! You'll be tested on:

  • Vocabulary: 10 words from Days 151-155
  • Reading Comprehension: Questions about the chapters
  • Grammar: Adjectives and Adjective Order
  • Writing: Informative Writing

Time needed: About 45-60 minutes

Tip: Take your time and do your best work!

Page 3

Vocabulary Assessment

Write the definition for each word. Use complete sentences.

1. delightful

2. humble

3. radiant

4. miracle

5. terrific

6. solemn

7. earnest

8. whisper

9. struggle

10. comfort

Page 4

Reading Comprehension Test

Answer these questions about the chapters you read this week:

Question 1:

[Comprehension question about this week's chapters]

Question 2:

[Comprehension question about this week's chapters]

Question 3:

[Comprehension question about this week's chapters]

Question 4:

[Comprehension question about this week's chapters]

Question 5:

[Comprehension question about this week's chapters]

Page 5

Grammar Assessment

Adjectives and Adjective Order

Write three sentences with multiple adjectives describing characters or places from the chapter.

Page 6

Writing Assessment

Informative Writing

Prompt:

Describe what life was like for Dorothy in Kansas before the cyclone. Include at least 3 specific details.

Requirements: Write a paragraph explaining something from the story. Include clear details and examples.

Page 7

Assessment Complete!

Great work on your assessment! You've completed 5 full days of Heidi.

See you on Day 156!
